title: Duplicate CI Name Detection
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:cmdb_ci" earliest=-1d
| eval norm_name=lower(trim(name))
| where norm_name!="" AND isnotnull(norm_name)
| stats values(sys_id) as sys_ids, dc(sys_id) as distinct_ids, values(ci_class) as classes by norm_name
| where distinct_ids>1
| table norm_name, distinct_ids, classes, sys_ids
| sort -distinct_ids
description: Duplicate names break joins between incidents, changes, and monitoring; detecting duplicates early restores CMDB accuracy and reduces routing errors across ITSM processes.
value: Duplicate names break joins between incidents, changes, and monitoring; detecting duplicates early restores CMDB accuracy and reduces routing errors across ITSM processes.
implementation: (1) Extend normalization if your naming standard strips domains (`mvindex(split(name,"."),0)`); (2) Enrich with `serial_number` when names collide legitimately; (3) Feed results into a CMDB merge workflow and track `distinct_ids` trend monthly.
visualization: Table (duplicate name clusters), Single value (duplicate name count), Bar chart (duplicates by CI class).
knownFalsePositives: False duplicates and drift when naming rules change, discovery merges, or the same system has more than one nickname.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:cmdb_ci"` (`name`, `sys_id`, `ci_class`, `serial_number`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Extend normalization if your naming standard strips domains (`mvindex(split(name,"."),0)`); (2) Enrich with `serial_number` when names collide legitimately; (3) Feed results into a CMDB merge workflow and track `distinct_ids` trend monthly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:cmdb_ci" earliest=-1d
| eval norm_name=lower(trim(name))
| where norm_name!="" AND isnotnull(norm_name)
| stats values(sys_id) as sys_ids, dc(sys_id) as distinct_ids, values(ci_class) as classes by norm_name
| where distinct_ids>1
| table norm_name, distinct_ids, classes, sys_ids
| sort -distinct_ids
```

Understanding this SPL

**Duplicate CI Name Detection** — Duplicate names break joins between incidents, changes, and monitoring; detecting duplicates early restores CMDB accuracy and reduces routing errors across ITSM processes.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:cmdb_ci"` (`name`, `sys_id`, `ci_class`, `serial_number`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:cmdb_ci. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:cmdb_ci", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **norm_name** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where norm_name!="" AND isnotnull(norm_name)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by norm_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where distinct_ids>1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Duplicate CI Name Detection**): table norm_name, distinct_ids, classes, sys_ids
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ServiceNow configuration item or related CMDB view. Spot-check a few CIs, owners, and relationship fields, then confirm discovery dates if we measure freshness.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (duplicate name clusters), Single value (duplicate name count), Bar chart (duplicates by CI class).
grandmaExplanation: We look for the same name on more than one thing, so we do not direct work or a change to the wrong box.
